title: Don't Repeat Yourself - Loops and Efficiency
description: Students learn what loops are, why they make code efficient, and how to debug looping bugs.
learningObjectives: Explain what a loop is and why programmers use loops instead of repeating the same line many times.
Identify repetition in everyday routines and technology examples.
Use and explain key vocabulary: loop, iteration, bug, debug, efficient.
Spot a bug in a loop and suggest a fix.
prerequisites: Lesson 1 - students already understand algorithms and sequences.
materials: Paper and pencils for every student
Optional sticky notes or index cards for loop cards activity
Clear space at the front for physical activity
fullDescription: Age group: Groep 5-8 (ages 8-12). Duration: 45-60 minutes. Devices required: none (fully unplugged). SLO alignment: Domain B (22B). Builds on Lesson 1 by extending sequence thinking into repetition and efficiency. Role model: Grace Hopper, who helped build early programming languages and documented the first actual computer bug, giving us the terms bug and debug.

teacherInstructions.setup: Write vocabulary on the board and keep it visible all lesson: Loop, Iteration, Bug, Debug, Efficient. Prepare open floor space for the human loop and Fortnite dance debugging activity.
steps: Phase 1 (0-3 min): Spark question using the brushing-teeth scale example. Capture highest and lowest guesses and reveal why loops are needed.
Phase 2 (3-10 min): Human loop warm-up. Run Clap/Stamp/Beep sequence, then rewrite as LOOP 5 TIMES and discuss iteration counting and LOOP FOREVER.
Phase 3 (10-20 min): Concept intro and loop types. Connect to Lesson 1 sandwich sequence, show efficiency rewrite with LOOP 10 TIMES, and introduce count/until/forever loops at age-appropriate depth.
Phase 4 (20-42 min): Fortnite dance bug challenge. Run working loop, then buggy LOOP FOREVER version, then pair debugging with rule constraints and share fixes.
Phase 5 (42-47 min): Discussion on loop correctness, forever loops in real life, and precision versus efficiency.
Exercises (47-57 min): Complete Exercise 1 (Rewrite with a loop) and Exercise 2 (Spot the bug). Use extension for fast finishers.
Share and reflect (52-57 min): Students share corrected loops and complete sentence: 'A loop is useful when _____ and dangerous when _____.'
discussionPrompts: Round 1 worked and Round 2 broke. What changed between the two programs?
Where in real life does something loop forever, and what stops it?
Can code be both precise and efficient at the same time? Why or why not?
If loops make code shorter, why not use loops for everything?
tips: Do not over-focus on exact tooth-brushing math; absurd scale is the learning hook.
The physical loop is key for this age group. Keep it short but concrete.
For high-energy classes, let students mime actions seated instead of standing.
During pair debug work, ask guiding questions about end position rather than giving answers.
Leave the sugar loop question open: how does a machine know when to stop?

teacherGuide: Core message: loops reduce repetition while preserving precision. Students should experience both a working loop and a broken loop physically, then debug with peers. Observe whether students identify the exact source of failure (forever repeat and sequence state mismatch), and whether they can justify fixes clearly.

studentExercises.title: Exercise 1 - Rewrite It with a Loop
studentExercises.description: Rewrite the repeated hair-brushing algorithm using loops, then create a more complex loop for left/right/back sections. Keep output the same but code more efficient.
instructions: Start from this long algorithm: Move brush from top of head to bottom (repeated 10 times).
Rewrite it as one count loop that produces the same output.
Then write a second version: brush left side, brush right side, brush back; repeat those three sections three times.
Swap with a partner and check whether both loop versions produce the same result as intended.
If the output changes, debug and rewrite.
successCriteria: Student identifies the repeated unit correctly.
Loop version is shorter but equivalent in output.
Partner can explain why the rewritten loop works.

studentExercises.title: Exercise 2 - Spot the Bug
studentExercises.description: Analyze three buggy loops and write fixes: classroom movement logic, infinite sugar loop, and fridge resource leak.
instructions: Buggy loop 1: LOOP 5 TIMES: Stand up; Sit down; Lie on the floor. Decide whether this is a logic bug, sequence bug, or both, and justify your fix.
Buggy loop 2: LOOP FOREVER: Add one sugar to the coffee; Stir. Add a stop condition and discuss how a machine can detect it.
Buggy loop 3: LOOP 3 TIMES: Open fridge; Take out juice; Pour glass. Fix the missing close-fridge step and explain the resource leak problem.
Share one fix with another pair and test whether it removes the bug.
successCriteria: Students identify the core bug in all three loops.
At least one fix includes a clear stop condition.
Students can explain why the corrected version is safer or more correct.

studentExercises.title: Extension - Write a Loop for Something in Your Life
studentExercises.description: Choose a repeated real-life activity and model it as a loop with type, stop condition, and one possible bug plus fix.
instructions: Choose one repeated activity from your life.
Name the loop type: count, until, or forever.
Write the steps inside the loop.
Describe what stops the loop, or explain why it never stops.
Find one possible bug and write a fix.
Challenge: write a loop with another loop inside it (nested loop).
successCriteria: Student can identify and repair one plausible bug.

studentChallenges.title: Fortnite Dance Bug Debug
studentChallenges.description: Debug the dance loop so it keeps all three moves, ends correctly, and stays physically executable every iteration.
instructions: Run working code: LOOP 3 TIMES: Dab, Spin, Jump.
Run buggy code: LOOP FOREVER: Dab, Spin, Touch your toes.
In pairs, rewrite code so it has a stop and remains physically possible each iteration.
Keep all three dance moves in your corrected version.
Share and defend your debug choices.
successCriteria: Corrected loop includes a finite stop.
Sequence can be executed repeatedly without getting stuck.
Pair explains why fix works better than original buggy version.

studentChallenges.title: Infinite Loop Rescue
studentChallenges.description: Convert a dangerous forever loop into a safe until or count loop with a measurable stop condition.
instructions: Choose one forever loop example from class.
Rewrite it as either a count loop or an until loop.
State exactly how the program can detect the stop condition.
Test your stop condition by giving a counterexample.
Revise if your loop still risks running forever.
successCriteria: Stop condition is explicit and measurable.
Group can explain how the machine checks stop status.
Rewritten loop avoids infinite repetition in the test case.
